turns.content: I'm trying to compress a large binary log file and I assume I should just wrap my BufReader in flate2::Read::new, similar to how I used std::io::BufReader before. Does that work for reading compressed streams?

That approach won't work because flate2::Read wraps a stream but doesn't handle the decompression logic internally; you need to pass an already-compressed reader into it or use a wrapper that handles both. For reading compressed files, you should wrap your File in flate2::read::GzDecoder instead of wrapping a BufReader directly.
Got it, but what if I want to stream decompress without loading the whole file into memory? Can I combine flate2 with tokio::fs::File for async streaming?

Yes, you can chain tokio::fs::File with flate2's GzDecoder to get an async stream that decompresses on-the-fly. You'll need to implement a trait or use the crate's built-in traits so your async runtime can handle the buffer without blocking.
